explanation.rule: Negative prefixes are added to the beginning of words to create opposite or negative meanings.

groups.note: The prefix dis- creates the opposite meaning and is commonly added to verbs, nouns, and adjectives. It often implies removal, reversal, or a negative state.

examples.definition: to have a different opinion from someone
not telling the truth; not trustworthy
a lack of confidence or belief in someone
not happy or pleased with something
to fail to meet someone's hopes or expectations

wordForms.definition: to have a different or opposite opinion
wordForms.example: The two experts strongly disagree on the best course of action.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to have the same opinion as someone

wordForms.definition: to have an unfavourable opinion about something
wordForms.example: Her parents disapprove of her decision to leave university.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to have a favourable opinion about something

wordForms.definition: a feeling of dissatisfaction or unhappiness
wordForms.example: Growing discontent among workers led to a strike.
wordForms.rootDefinition: satisfaction and happiness with a situation

wordForms.definition: to break the connection between two things
wordForms.example: Please disconnect the power cable before opening the device.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to join or link two things together

wordForms.definition: not telling the truth; not trustworthy
wordForms.example: It would be dishonest to claim the work was entirely your own.
wordForms.rootDefinition: telling the truth; free of deceit

wordForms.definition: a state of confusion or lack of organisation
wordForms.example: The office was in complete disorder after the move.

wordForms.definition: a lack of respect or polite behaviour
wordForms.example: Talking over the speaker is a form of disrespect.

wordForms.definition: not pleased or happy with something
wordForms.example: Customers were dissatisfied with the quality of the service.
wordForms.rootDefinition: pleased and content with a result

wordForms.definition: a lack of confidence or belief in someone or something
wordForms.example: There is widespread distrust of the new policy among staff.
wordForms.rootDefinition: firm belief in the reliability of someone or something

wordForms.definition: to fail to meet the hopes or expectations of someone
wordForms.example: I don't want to disappoint my parents after all their support.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to select someone for a role or position

multipleChoiceExercises.sentence: The manager and the team ___ on the best approach to take.
Her parents ___ of her plan to travel abroad alone.
The office was in complete ___ when we arrived on Monday morning.
She felt ___ with the outcome and decided to appeal.
Spreading false information is deeply ___ behaviour.
Growing ___ among voters led to a sharp fall in turnout.

transformExercises.sentence: The results of the study ___ with previously published findings. (AGREE)
You must ___ the router before attempting any repairs. (CONNECT)
The news was bound to ___ those who had expected a pay rise. (APPOINT)
There is a growing ___ of politicians among the public. (TRUST)
Treating a colleague rudely is a sign of ___. (RESPECT)
